title: About Me
text: Background

title: About Me
text:
Personal Statement
Hello! I’m a Marketing Analytics student at Cal Poly Pomona with a genuine passion for data and the stories it can tell. My academic background spans quantitative research methods, consumer behavior, and digital marketing strategy — and I’ve spent the past two years applying those frameworks to real datasets.
I believe the most valuable analysts are those who can move fluidly between the technical and the strategic, equally comfortable in a boardroom presenting insights to a non-technical audience. That’s the profession I’m building toward. Additionally, I would like the opportunity to work for local government. I believe a strong local government helps foster a stronger sense of community.
What I bring to a team:

Strong foundation in EDA, regression modeling, and customer segmentation
Experience presenting data-driven recommendations to diverse stakeholders
Fluency across the analytics stack: R, Python, SQL, Tableau, Google Analytics
A collaborative, curious disposition — I learn fast and ask good questions

Career Objective: I’m seeking full-time or internship opportunities in marketing analytics, data science, or digital strategy. I’m particularly drawn to roles that blend quantitative rigor with consumer insight. I am also seeking data analysis positions, both full-time and part-time.

title: Marketing Dashboard
text:
Monthly Conversions — R Chart


Code
library(tidyverse)

conversions <- tibble(
  month = factor(
    c("Oct", "Nov", "Dec", "Jan", "Feb", "Mar"),
    levels = c("Oct", "Nov", "Dec", "Jan", "Feb", "Mar")
  ),
  value    = c(210, 290, 340, 280, 410, 380),
  highlight = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)
)

ggplot(conversions, aes(x = month, y = value, fill = highlight)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(value)), vjust = -0.5,
            size = 3.2, family = "sans", color = "#0f2444") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = "#0f2444", `TRUE` = "#c9a84c"),
                    guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12)),
                     labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Conversions",
       caption = "Gold bar = peak month (February)") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "sans") +
  theme(
    axis.text  = element_text(color = "#5a5a5a", size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(color = "#0f2444", size = 10),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    plot.caption = element_text(color = "#888", size = 8)
  )





Monthly conversions across all channels, Q1 2025. February spike driven by organic search content refresh.

title: Marketing Dashboard
text:
Channel Mix — R Pie / Donut


Code
channels <- tibble(
  channel = c("Organic Search", "Paid Social", "Email", "Direct"),
  pct     = c(42, 28, 18, 12)
) |>
  mutate(
    channel = fct_reorder(channel, pct, .desc = TRUE),
    label   = paste0(channel, "\n", pct, "%")
  )

ggplot(channels, aes(x = 2, y = pct, fill = channel)) +
  geom_col(width = 1, color = "white", linewidth = 0.8) +
  coord_polar(theta = "y", start = 0) +
  xlim(0.5, 2.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Organic Search" = "#0f2444",
    "Paid Social"    = "#c9a84c",
    "Email"          = "#1a3a6b",
    "Direct"         = "#b0a080"
  )) +
  labs(fill = "Channel") +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans") +
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        legend.text = element_text(size = 9, color = "#1c1c1c"))





Q1 2025 channel mix by conversion volume. Organic search is the top driver.










Key Insight: Organic search conversions grew 34% in February following the content refresh initiative — the strongest ROI of any channel this quarter. Recommend increasing SEO investment in Q2.

Quick Profile


Who I Am

I’m a Marketing Analytics student at Cal Poly Pomona with a passion for turning complex data into clear, actionable insights. I combine technical skills in R and Python with a strategic marketing mindset — helping organizations make better, faster decisions.
My work spans exploratory data analysis, customer segmentation, campaign performance modeling, and interactive dashboard development. I’m driven by curiosity: every dataset is a story waiting to be told.

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
About This Presentation
This presentation walks through the full EDA pipeline applied to the RPE (Rate of Perceived Exertion) combined survey dataset.
Goals:

Load and inspect a labelled SPSS dataset
Apply data cleaning and standardization
Perform exploratory data analysis
Generate visualizations and initial insights

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
Loading Libraries and Data

# Uncomment to install if needed:
# install.packages(c("tidyverse","haven","labelled","janitor","gt","sjPlot"))

library(tidyverse)
library(haven)
library(labelled)
library(janitor)
library(gt)
library(sjPlot)

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
Reading and Printing Data

data <- read_sav("../data/RPE-Combined-2019-12-19.sav")
data <- data |> clean_names()
data







Tip


clean_names() from janitor converts all column names to snake_case, removing spaces and special characters — essential for consistent downstream coding.

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
Data Structure and Summary

str(data)
summary(data)
glimpse(data)

# Data dictionary — shows SPSS variable labels
view_df(data)

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
What glimpse() Tells Us
glimpse() gives a quick transposed view of the dataset — perfect for a first look when you have many columns.







Output
What to look for




Row / column count
Dataset size, potential performance issues


Column types
<dbl>, <chr>, <fct>, <lbl> (labelled)


First values
Obvious encoding issues, unexpected values

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
The labelled Package
SPSS .sav files store variable labels and value labels that R’s read_sav() preserves as <labelled> vectors.

# Inspect labels on one variable
data |> pull(rpe_score) |> val_labels()   # replace with actual var name

# Convert labelled vars to factors for plotting
data_clean <- data |>
  mutate(across(where(is.labelled), as_factor))







Note


Always decide early whether to work with labelled vectors or convert to factors — mixing both leads to downstream issues.

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
EDA Overview






Tip


In this section we apply EDA techniques to:

Summarize key characteristics (mean, median, spread)
Uncover patterns and trends
Detect anomalies and outliers
Assess data quality (missing values, encoding issues)
Generate insights that guide modeling and decisions

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
Descriptive Statistics

# Frequency tables for categorical variables
data |>
  count(gender, sort = TRUE) |>   # replace with actual variable name
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100)

# Summary statistics for numeric variables
data |>
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric),
    list(mean = mean, sd = sd, median = median, min = min, max = max),
    na.rm = TRUE
  ))

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
Distribution Visualization

data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = rpe_score)) +     # replace with actual variable name
  geom_histogram(fill = "#0f2444", color = "#c9a84c",
                 bins = 20, alpha = 0.9) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of RPE Scores",
       x = "RPE Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "sans")

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
RPE Project In-Class Activities
RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
Key Takeaways


Data Quality

X% of records complete (no missing values)
Y variables required re-encoding from labelled format
Z outliers flagged for review


Substantive Patterns

RPE scores show a bimodal distribution — possible sub-population
Several Likert items exhibit ceiling effects (>70% at max)
Strong correlation between [var A] and [var B] (r = 0.XX)

title: RPE Project In-Class Activities
text:
Recommended Next Steps

Deeper missing data analysis — investigate whether missingness is random (MAR) or systematic (MNAR)
Sub-group analysis — split by demographic variable to explore bimodal RPE pattern
Feature engineering — create composite scores from correlated Likert items
Predictive modeling — logistic or OLS regression on key outcome variable

title: Analytics Projects
text:
My Work
A collection of analytics projects completed during my studies at Cal Poly Pomona and independently. Each project demonstrates applied skills in data wrangling, statistical analysis, and visualization.

title: Analytics Projects
section: Project 1 — RPE Survey Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
Project 1 — RPE Survey Exploratory Data Analysis






Course: IBM 3202 · Cal Poly Pomona
Tools: R · tidyverse · haven · labelled · janitor · gt · sjPlot
Date: April 2026




Overview
This project applied a full exploratory data analysis (EDA) pipeline to the RPE (Rate of Perceived Exertion) combined survey dataset, originally collected and stored in SPSS .sav format. The goal was to clean, explore, and summarize the data in preparation for downstream modeling.


Loading Libraries and Data


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(haven)
library(labelled)
library(janitor)
library(gt)
library(sjPlot)




Code
data <- read_sav("../data/RPE-Combined-2019-12-19.sav")
data <- data |> clean_names()
glimpse(data)




Data Structure and Summary


Code
str(data)
summary(data)

# Data dictionary — shows variable labels from SPSS
view_df(data)




Key Findings






EDA Takeaways
Applying EDA techniques here surfaced three notable patterns: (1) several Likert-scale variables were right-skewed, suggesting ceiling effects; (2) missing data was concentrated in a small subset of survey items; (3) RPE scores clustered around two distinct modes, hinting at a latent sub-population difference worth investigating in modeling.





Code
# Example: distribution of a key RPE variable
data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = rpe_score)) +   # replace with actual variable name
  geom_histogram(fill = "#0f2444", color = "#c9a84c", bins = 20) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of RPE Scores",
       x = "RPE Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "sans") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#0f2444"))

title: Analytics Projects
text:
Project 2 — Customer Segmentation Study






Type: Independent Project
Tools: Python · pandas · scikit-learn · matplotlib · seaborn
Date: March 2026




Overview
Using k-means clustering on transactional and demographic data, I identified four distinct customer segments. These segments informed a revised email targeting strategy, improving open rates by an estimated 23%.


Methodology

Data Preparation — normalized RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) features, imputed missing income values using median imputation by region.
Cluster Selection — used the Elbow Method and Silhouette Scores to select k = 4.
Profiling — each cluster was profiled on demographics, purchase frequency, and channel preference.
Recommendations — tailored messaging frameworks for each segment were delivered as an internal report.



Segment Profiles









Segment
Label
Key Trait
Recommended Action




1
High-Value Loyalists
High frequency, high spend
Retention rewards program


2
At-Risk Customers
Declining recency
Win-back email campaign


3
New Acquirers
Recent first purchase
Onboarding nurture sequence


4
Bargain Seekers
Promo-driven, low CLV
Margin-conscious promotions

title: Analytics Projects
text:
Project 3 — Campaign Performance Dashboard






Type: Course Project
Tools: Tableau Public · Google Analytics 4 · Excel
Date: February 2026




Overview
Built an interactive multi-channel marketing dashboard aggregating performance data from paid social, email, and organic search. Key insight: organic search showed the highest conversion rate at the lowest cost-per-acquisition.


Metrics Tracked

  
    Total Reach
    142K
    ▲ 18% vs prior period
  
  
    Conversions
    3,841
    ▲ 11% vs prior period
  
  
    Email Open Rate
    28.4%
    ▼ 2% vs prior period
  
  
    Cost Per Lead
    $14.20
    ▲ Improved 9%
